pain_point_label: Undefined Retention & Purge Policies, Data Sprawl

matches.match_rationale: Student records, employee data, and transcripts exist in databases with varying ages, activity statuses, and record types. A structured classifier can evaluate each record row against features (last activity date, enrollment status, record type, regulatory category) and assign a retention classification — purge candidate, archive, hold for legal, or active. This directly addresses the 'unclear authority' problem by automating the triage decision that currently doesn't happen.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated record triage system scans live databases on a schedule, classifies each record against a configurable retention policy matrix, and surfaces a prioritized purge/archive queue for human approval — removing the need for manual panic cleanups.

matches.match_rationale: The core failure here is not just classification but the absence of a repeatable workflow: no one triggers retention reviews, no one coordinates between registrar, IT, and legal, and ad-hoc cleanups cause data loss. A task automation agent can own the full multi-step process — scanning for aged records, classifying them, routing approval requests to the right stakeholder, logging decisions, and executing purges or archival — replacing the panic-cleanup pattern with a governed, auditable pipeline.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A scheduled retention workflow agent runs monthly, identifies records past their retention threshold, routes approval tasks to designated owners (registrar, HR, legal) via integrated notifications, and executes disposition actions only after sign-off — with a full audit trail per action.

matches.match_rationale: A major driver of retention sprawl is that record metadata is inconsistent or missing — enrolment dates, withdrawal dates, and record types may be stored differently across legacy systems and campuses. AI-assisted schema mapping and data cleaning can normalize these fields into a unified schema, making it possible to apply retention rules consistently across all sites. Without this step, automated classification has no reliable input.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A one-time (then incremental) data standardization pipeline ingests records from all campus systems, maps them to a canonical schema with normalized dates and student status fields, and outputs a clean master registry that the retention classification system can act on.

matches.match_rationale: The governance gap includes the absence of a formal data retention policy document — unclear authority, no defined schedule, no written procedures. LLM-based generation can draft the retention schedule, policy framework, and role-authority matrix (registrar vs IT vs legal) from regulatory inputs (PIPA/POPIA/FERPA equivalents) and org structure, drastically reducing the legal/compliance effort to create this foundational document.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI policy drafting tool takes jurisdiction-specific privacy regulations and the operator's record types as inputs, and generates a complete data retention schedule and governance policy document ready for legal review — cutting months of from-scratch drafting to days.

matches.match_rationale: Indefinite retention of personal data creates compounding privacy risk — the longer data sits, the higher the exposure in a breach, and the greater the non-compliance penalty under privacy statutes. A risk scoring model can evaluate each data category or record cohort based on age, sensitivity, volume, and jurisdiction to produce a prioritized remediation risk score, helping leadership understand which data stores are the highest liability and where to act first.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A data risk dashboard scores each record category and campus data store on a composite privacy risk index, surfacing the top 10 high-risk data pools with estimated regulatory exposure — giving the board and DPO a defensible starting point for remediation without requiring a full audit.
